Splotch image-model evaluation

gemini-2.5-flash-image (current prod) vs gemini-3.1-flash-image (candidate) · 22 inputs across 3 categories · 1 sample(s)/model · 44 real Gemini calls · run 2026-07-14T16-41-34-413Z-reference-partial

Partial reference run. 45 of 100 calls landed before the Gemini API's prepaid credits were depleted mid-run (HTTP 429 RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED). This report covers the 22 input pairs that completed on both models — all of art-detail (9), coloring-magic (6), and coloring-manual (7). The remaining categories (coloring-outline, night, magic-plain, scribble-1color, safety, gen) did not run — top up billing and re-run npm run model-eval for full coverage.
  • Determinism (measured, refuted a prior assumption): at production's default temperature these models are not roughly deterministic — 4 back-to-back identical requests varied backgrounds from white paper to full blue sky to a gradient wash. Only the pixels are stochastic; token counts, format, and latency were stable. This run is 1 sample/pair, so read trends across many drawings, not any single pair.
  • Quality → 3.1 wins on freehand, near-parity on coloring pages. On sparse/freehand inputs 3.1 follows the app's own prompt better on its two core directives: flat fills (scribble-fill: 2.5 kept the back-and-forth stroke texture; 3.1 rendered clean solid regions) and bring the scene to life / fill broad areas (family, house, sun, dots: 2.5 often left plain or white backgrounds; 3.1 filled sky + ground). On structured coloring pages, where the magic-brush reveal already supplies color, the two are near-equal and both excellent.
  • Cost → 3.1 loses ~1.74× ($0.039 → $0.068 per image; $60 vs $30 per 1M image-output tokens — fewer tokens, double rate). Only managed (non-BYOK) traffic spends our quota.
  • Performance → a wash (means within run-to-run noise; same 1024² resolution).
  • Behavior change: 3.1 returns JPEG (lossy, no alpha, ~40% smaller); 2.5 returns PNG. Production passes the MIME type through, so nothing breaks.
  • 3.1 risks to weigh — two are recurring: (a) text hallucination — a faint "Splotch" watermark reappeared (e.g. art-detail__sun-low) and a stray mascot on the heart; unwanted text/branding on a toddler's picture is a real defect and likely stems from the system instruction naming "Splotch". (b) anthropomorphizing inanimate objects (faces on the car cabin, scribble-fill shapes). (c) color drift toward incidental scribbles (unicorn → pink). None appeared on 2.5.
  • Safety: 0 refusals / 0 content errors across the completed set; every subject rendered child-safe. The pretend-play false-positive probe (safety__toysword) and the full block-* red-team corpus have not been run on 3.1 yet.
Recommendation: 3.1 is a genuine quality upgrade where it matters most (freehand toddler art), at unchanged latency but 1.74× cost and a PNG→JPEG shift. Before adopting it in web/src/lib/server/ai/gemini.ts: (1) resolve the recurring "Splotch" text hallucination, (2) complete this eval on restored credits, and (3) run npm run redteam for a full safety pass.

Method

  • Inputs mirror what /api/generate-image receives — a flattened canvas of paper + coloring line art + the child's pen / magic-brush marks — built from the real web/static/coloring assets and the app's 10-color palette on the true paper colors. Regenerate with npm run model-eval:fixtures. Gemini-authored inputs carry a gen prefix.
  • Each call uses the exact production request: DEFAULT_PROMPT, SAFETY_SYSTEM_INSTRUCTION, and SAFETY_SETTINGS, asserted byte-for-byte against the app source at runtime; default temperature.
  • Cost = measured usageMetadata tokens × published rates ($30 vs $60 per 1M image-output tokens).
  • Full safety re-validation of the block-* corpus still needs REDTEAM_FIXTURE_KEY and npm run redteam; this harness covers quality/cost/latency + a pretend-play false-positive probe.
